text: The mechanisms for noncompetitive picrotoxin site binding-induced reduction in chloride conductance have been investigated intensively for several decades. The consensus has been reached with ample support of computational and experimental evidence. Noncompetitive channel blockers fit the 2' to 9' pore region forming hydrogen bonds with the T6' hydroxyl and hydrophobic interactions with A2', T6' and L9' alkyl substituents (Chen et al. 2006), which is the primary binding site in the chloride channel lumen lined by five TM2 segments, thereby blocking the channel. Recent evidence suggests that there also exists a secondary modulatory pocket at the interface between the ligand-binding domain and the transmembrane domain of the iGABAR (Carpenter et al. 2013). It is believed that the two mechanisms mediate the blockage of chloride conductance (Yoon et al. 1993; Carpenter et al. 2013).
